coredata.dc:title: Carbon quantum dots modified small molecular quinone salt as cathode materials for sodium-ion batteries
coredata.dc:creator: Ding, Youchi
Xie, Lingling
Zhang, Yuwei
Chen, Xizhuo
Niu, Yu
Xu, Jing
Han, Qing
Qiu, Xuejing
Miao, Yongxia
Xiao, Yongmei
Zhu, Limin
Cao, Xiaoyu
coredata.dc:description: Organic compounds are considered auspicious due to their widespread presence in nature, low cost, and sustainability. However, the inherent drawbacks of these organics’ compounds, like low-conductivity and high-solubility, restrict their large-scale application. To solve these problems, we innovatively combined carbon quantum dots (CDs) with active substances (tetrahydroxybenzoquinone ortho-disodium salt (o-Na2THBQ)) at different mass ratios. The optimized o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 (the mass ratio of o-Na2THBQ to CDs is 10:2) exhibited excellent electrochemical performance, the first-cycle discharge specific capacity was 410 mAh g−1 and maintained 182 mAh g−1 after 500 cycles, and the Coulombic efficiency was nearly 100 %. After 1300 cycles, the o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 composite demonstrated a high specific capacity of 63 mAh g−1 at 500 mA g−1. This work provides a reference to design an optimal composite ratio of carbon dots (CDs) and organics to fabricate cathode materials for high-performance sodium-ion batteries.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Sodium-ion batteries
Cathode materials
o-Na2THBQ/CDs composites
Electrochemical performances

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation, China (Nos. 52071132 , U21A20284 and U1904216 ), Zhongyuan Thousand People Plan-The Zhongyuan Youth Talent Support Program (in Science and Technology), China (No. ZYQR201810139 ), Innovative Funds Plan of Henan University of Technology, China (No. 2020ZKCJ04 ), and Natural Science Foundation of Henan, China (No. 222300420138 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD, (b) FT-IR, and (c) TG patterns of o-Na2THBQ, and o-Na2THBQ/CDs composites; SEM images of o-Na2THBQ (d), o-Na2THBQ/CDs-1 (e), o-Na2THBQ/CDs-3 (f); (g)−(i) SEM images of o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2.

BET diagram of o-Na2THBQ (a), and o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 (b); (c) BET comparison chart of o-Na2THBQ, o-Na2THBQ/CDs-1, o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 and o-Na2THBQ/CDs-3; (d)−(f) XPS full-spectrum and elemental fitting of o-Na2THBQ; (g)−(i) XPS full-spectrum and elemental fitting of o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2.

Cyclic voltammetry curves of o-Na2THBQ (a), o-Na2THBQ/CDs-1 (b), o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 (c), and o-Na2THBQ/CDs-3 (d); The charge–discharge curves of the first five cycles of o-Na2THBQ (e) and o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 (f); (g) Cyclic voltammetry curves of o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 at different scan rates, and (h)−(i) the linear relationship between I p and v 1/2.

Selected charge–discharge curves of o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 at the current density of 500 mA g−1 (a) and 50 mA g−1 (b); (c) Charge-discharge curves of o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 at different current densities; (d) Long cycle performance of o-Na2THBQ and o-Na2THBQ/CDs composites at the current density of 50 mA g−1; (e) Long cycle performance o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 at the current density of 500 mA g−1; (f) Step rate performance of o-Na2THBQ and o-Na2THBQ/CDs composites.

(a) Capacity-voltage curves of o-Na2THBQ and o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 in the fifth cycle; (b), (e) Time-voltage curves of o-Na2THBQ and o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2; (c), (f) Diffusion coefficients of Na+ ions of o-Na2THBQ and o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2; (d) Voltage-time curves of a single GITT titration at 3.0 V.
(a) Charge-discharge curves of o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2; (b) ex-situ IR images of o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 at different voltages; (c) o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 schematic diagram of charging and discharging.

head.ce:title: Carbon quantum dots modified small molecular quinone salt as cathode materials for sodium-ion batteries

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University of Technology, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Henan University of Technology, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Environmental Engineering, Henan University of Technology, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Environmental Engineering, Henan University of Technology, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of High Specific Energy Materials for Electrochemical Power Sources of Zhengzhou City, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of High Specific Energy Materials for Electrochemical Power Sources of Zhengzhou City

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of High Specific Energy Materials for Electrochemical Power Sources of Zhengzhou City, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding authors at: Key Laboratory of High Specific Energy Materials for Electrochemical Power Sources of Zhengzhou City, Zhengzhou 450001, PR China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of High Specific Energy Materials for Electrochemical Power Sources of Zhengzhou City

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Organic compounds are considered auspicious due to their widespread presence in nature, low cost, and sustainability. However, the inherent drawbacks of these organics’ compounds, like low-conductivity and high-solubility, restrict their large-scale application. To solve these problems, we innovatively combined carbon quantum dots (CDs) with active substances (tetrahydroxybenzoquinone ortho-disodium salt (o-Na2THBQ)) at different mass ratios. The optimized o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 (the mass ratio of o-Na2THBQ to CDs is 10:2) exhibited excellent electrochemical performance, the first-cycle discharge specific capacity was 410 mAh g−1 and maintained 182 mAh g−1 after 500 cycles, and the Coulombic efficiency was nearly 100 %. After 1300 cycles, the o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 composite demonstrated a high specific capacity of 63 mAh g−1 at 500 mA g−1. This work provides a reference to design an optimal composite ratio of carbon dots (CDs) and organics to fabricate cathode materials for high-performance sodium-ion batteries.

ce:section.ce:para: Over time, fossil fuel consumption has had a significant impact on the environment including global warming and environmental pollution, and many irreversible ecological pollution problems have become a world-recognized concern [1–4]. Thus, developing environmentally friendly future energy storage systems is crucial [5]. Typical representatives of clean renewable energy include solar power, tidal energy, wind energy, and biomass energy, and so on. Storage and utilization of these new types of energies safely and effectively is an urgent challenge for modern-day researchers [6]. Lithium-ion batteries with high specific capacity and rapid charge–discharge characteristics are superior electrochemical energy storage technologies [7–11]. However, limited lithium resources and expensive costs constrain its development, making sodium-on batteries the best choice for high-performance energy storage [12–14]. The working principles of the two battery systems also have a lot of similarities. However, sodium content on the earth is relatively large, which can realize long-term recycling, thereby, enhancing the focus on sodium-ion batteries as a common energy storage material. Compared with ordinary inorganic materials, the advantage of organic conjugated carbonyl compound electrode materials is the high specific capacity, structural diversity, and fast reaction kinetics [15–18]. The electrochemical reaction mechanism is as follows: each oxygen atom on the carbonyl gets an electron during discharge, and at the same time intercalates sodium ions to form enolate sodium salt; during charging, the sodium ions are removed and the carbonyl groups are reduced, which is realized by the conversion between the carbonyl group and the enolate structure. Carbonyl compounds exhibit high electrochemical reversibility owing to the fast kinetics of the enolization reaction, with reversible (de)intercalation of Na+ [19–21]. In a typical electrochemical process, the single- or multi-electron reversible reaction takes place, producing a high specific capacity [22–25]. A common organic compound employed in sodium-ion batteries is tetrahydroxybenzoquinone (THBQ), and the carbonyl group in this material undergoes redox reactions during charging and discharging processes. Hence, THBQ is a very suitable candidate for future cathode in sodium-ion batteries [26,27]. However, the electron transport ability of THBQ is relatively poor because of the low conjugation. In addition, due to its high solubility in organic compounds, THBQ inevitably suffers from capacity loss and low-rate capability. Therefore, researchers have designed different methods, including polymerizing [28], converting small organic molecules into respective salts [29], or composited organic compounds with conductive carbon materials [30–33], and so on. As expected, we prepared o-Na2THBQ, which improved electrochemical properties compared with THBQ, but the long-term cycle performance and capacity of o-Na2THBQ at high current density are still not satisfactory. Carbon dots (CDs) are commonly used as composited agents for electrode materials due to their surface composition and suitable structure. For example, amino groups are capable of pseudo-capability, carboxyl and hydroxyl groups help to improve the wettability between electrode material and electrolyte, and quantum size can modulate crystal formation [34,35]. Based on this, we innovatively prepared a series of composites of CDs with o-Na2THBQ, which have several unique merits: First, the presence of ester bonds inhibits the dissolution of o-Na2THBQ molecules in electrolytes. Then, in the o-Na2THBQ composited electrodes, CDs served as continuous electron transport paths for o-Na2THBQ to enhance electrochemical performance. Finally, carbonyl groups on CDs contribute to electrochemical capacity as well as facilitate electron transfer in the electrodes.

According to previous literature reports, the synthesis of dihydroxybenzoquinone ortho-disodium salt (o-Na2THBQ) is as follows [36]: 50 % sodium carbonate (Na2CO3, chemically pure, Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., ltd) solution was added dropwise (approximately 1 mL) to 5 mL of inositol (C6H6O6, chemically pure, Shanghai Aladdin Bio-Chem Technology Co., ltd) until the yellow color of the solution disappeared completely. Then, 6 mL Na2CO3 solution was quickly added to the above solution, and dark purple precipitates appeared. Filter the precipitate and the filtrate was washed with deionized water. Wash again with ethanol until the color of the filtrate colorless. Subsequently, the precipitate was placed in a petri dish, in order to finally get a brown product, place it in a vacuum oven with the temperature adjusted to 80 °C until it is completely dry.

Add 0.04 g of CDs to 60 mL of absolute ethanol and sonicate the mixed solution for 30 min to form a homogeneous CDs solution. Subsequently, 0.2 g of o-Na2THBQ powder was added to the CDs solution prepared in the previous step, and it was placed in a magnetic sub to stir for 30 min. The solution was evaporated to complete dryness in a rotary evaporator. Repeat the same operation 3 times. To get the optimal ratio of o-Na2THBQ/CDs composites, we prepared three composites, named o-Na2THBQ/CDs-1 (mass ratio of 10:1), o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 (mass ratio of 10:2), and o-Na2THBQ/CDs-3 (mass ratio of 10:3), respectively.

X-ray powder diffraction (XRD) experiments were performed on a Rigaku MiniFlex 600 instrument. The Fourier transform infrared reflection (FT-IR) test was conducted by Spectrum 400F, a wavelength range of 4000–400 cm−1. The instrument for thermogravimetric-differential scanning calorimetry (TG-DSC) was a Netzsch Sta 449F3 thermogravimetric analyzer. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was tested using a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD instrument, where the X-ray source was Al-Kα. The microstructure of the samples was analyzed by a scanning electron microscope (SEM) with the designation FEI-Quanta 250 FEG instrument.

Mix the o-Na2THBQ/CDs composite with ketjen black (KB) and polyvinylidene fluoride (PVDF, 5 wt%) in NMP solution at a mass ratio of 7:2:1. The mixed materials were fully stirred for 24 h. Then spread the mixed solution transferred to the aluminum foil with a coating rod, put it in the oven (YB-1A), adjust the temperature to 80 °C, and cut into 1 cm diameter discs with a microtome (MSK-T10). The glove box is filled with high-purity argon gas to assemble the battery. A coin-type battery (CR2016) was used to assemble the sodium-ion battery. The sodium sheet is used as the anode, and the electrolyte is 1 M NaClO4 in EC/DEC (v: v = 1: 1). The constant current charge and discharge test was performed on a CT-4008 T battery tester (Shenzhen Newell Electronics Co., ltd.). Cyclic voltammetry was conducted using a CHI660D electrochemical workstation (Shanghai Chenhua Instrument Co., ltd.) with a scan rate of 0.1 mV s−1 and a scan voltage range of 1.0–3.0 V.

The structures of o-Na2THBQ, o-Na2THBQ/CDs-1, o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 and o-Na2THBQ/CDs-3 were analyzed using XRD. Similar XRD patterns for all materials (Fig. 1 (a)), the original structure of o-Na2THBQ did not change after the addition of CDs for complexation. Sharp diffraction peaks appeared at 2θ = 13.5°, 22.94°, 26.48°, 31.06°, 40.7°, and 44.1°, indicating the good crystallinity of the small molecules after compositing, facilitating the electrochemical performance of materials. Fig. 1(b) showed the FI-IR spectrum of the composites, depicting a strong absorption peak of the CONa bond at 1063 cm−1. The characteristic stretching vibrations of the CO and CC on the conjugated ring are embodied as absorption peaks at 1625 cm−1 and 1480 cm−1 [15], respectively. The absorption peak at 760 cm−1 of the fingerprint area is unique to the adjacent binary substitution of the benzene ring substitution area. The absorption peak at 1196 cm−1 is the characteristic absorption peak of CO. The results revealed that the structural features of all four materials are consistent. TG curves in Fig. 1(c) showed that all four materials decomposed at around 300 °C, and the decomposition rate is the fastest at 350–400 °C and tends to be stable at 650 °C, with a remaining mass of about 60 %. Comparing these TG curves, o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 composite maintained excellent thermal stability. At 800 °C, the remaining mass of o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 was 58 %. The microstructures of o-Na2THBQ, o-Na2THBQ/CDs-1, o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2, and o-Na2THBQ/CDs-3 were observed by SEM. The irregular block size of o-Na2THBQ was about 1 µm indicated a little agglomeration phenomenon (Fig. 1(d)), which diffusion and migration of ions are suppressed during the cycle. Fig. 1(e)−(f) are the SEM images of o-Na2THBQ/CDs-1 and o-Na2THBQ/CDs-3. A large number of irregular block-like agglomerations can be clearly seen. Fig. 1(g)−(i) showed the structure of o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2. The agglomeration phenomenon has eased slightly, suggesting that the sodium ions may have more migration and diffusion channels during the charge–discharge cycles, therefore electrochemical performance was significantly enhanced.

The specific surface areas of o-Na2THBQ and o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 can be clearly seen from Fig. 2 (a–b). The specific surface area of the three composites decreased with the increase in the proportion of composite CDs, as shown in Fig. 2(c). The specific surface areas of o-Na2THBQ/CDs-1, o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 and o-Na2THBQ/CDs-3 were 2.49, 2.10, and 2.04 m2 g−1, respectively. A progressive decrease in the specific surface area was caused by the CDs filled the pores of o-Na2THBQ. The higher the composited ratio, the higher the pore filled degree. However, the specific surface areas of o-Na2THBQ/CDs composites were not significantly reduced compared to the bulk o-Na2THBQ. Fig. 2(d) and (g) showed the full-spectrum of o-Na2THBQ and o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2, respectively, it can be seen that the main constituent elements and valence states of the materials have basically not changed before and after compositing. Fig. 2(e) and (f) were the C 1 s and O 1 s spectra of o-Na2THBQ, and the types of functional group peaks are exactly the same as those of o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 (Fig. 2(h) and (i)). As Fig. 2(h) showed, the C 1 s spectrum of o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2, peaks at 283.5, 284.8, 287.1, and 288.9 eV corresponded to CC, CC, CO, and CO bonds, respectively. In Fig. 3 (i), peaks at 529.8 and 531.4 eV corresponded to CO and CO bonds on the aromatic ring, respectively, and the Na-Auger peak at 534.4 eV indicated the oxygen element [37]. XPS spectra showed that introduction of CDs did not change the functional group species of o-Na2THBQ.

The electrochemical properties of o-Na2THBQ and o-Na2THBQ/CDs composites were measured by CV and charge–discharge tests. From this we can see many phenomena. Fig. 3(a) showed the first five CV curves of o-Na2THBQ at 0.2 mV s−1 and a voltage range of 1.0–3.0 V. o-Na2THBQ showed multiple pairs of redox peaks. The reduction peaks appeared at 1.27, 1.67, 1.86, 2.28, and 2.51 V, corresponding to the process of Na+ intercalation into carbonyl, CO to form a CONa bond, several oxidation peaks at 1.39, 1.92, 2.16, 2.38 and 2.67 V also appeared, corresponding to the removal of Na+ from CONa to form carbonyl CO again. The appearance of multiple pairs of redox peaks is attributed to both one-electron and two-electron reactions in o-Na2THBQ during the cycle [38]. Fig. 3(b–d) were the CV curves of the first five cycles of o-Na2THBQ/CDs-1, o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2, and o-Na2THBQ/CDs-3, respectively. The results showed that there was no obvious change in peak shape and peak position compared with the o-Na2THBQ material, suggesting that o-Na2THBQ composited with CDs has no direct effect on its redox reaction mechanism. More importantly, except for the first cycle, the CV curves of the following cycles basically overlapped in Fig. 3(a–d), indicating that o-Na2THBQ material has excellent structural reversibility and electrochemical cycling stability. However, from the intensity of the peaks, it can be seen that o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 material has the highest peak current, indicating that the material may have the highest specific charge–discharge capacity. The electrochemical sodium-ion storage capacity of electrode materials was determined through galvanostatic discharge/charge test and compared with the results of CV. Fig. 3(e) and (f) showed the charge–discharge curves of o-Na2THBQ and o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 at 50 mA g−1 for the first 5 cycles. The results in the figure indicated that there are multiple charge–discharge voltage plateaus. The voltage plateau is consistent with the peak shapes shown in the oxidation and reduction plots in the CV curves. However, the first loop is the exception, the charge–discharge curves of the o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 composite displayed better coincidence than those of the o-Na2THBQ material, indicating that o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 composite have better electrochemical stability. It should also be noted that since the battery has an initial voltage of about 2.08 V, the specific capacities of the two composites are low when the battery is charged to 3.0 V for the first time. The reason is that from 2.08 V to 3.0 V, the battery does not have sufficient voltage range for charging. Electrochemical kinetics of o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 were evaluated with CV test at the scan rates of 0.2–1.0 mV s−1. From this we can see many phenomena. Subsequent cycles have been increasing the sweep speed. As shown in Fig. 3(g), the reduction peak of the complex has been moving towards a lower potential, while the oxidation peak has shifted towards a higher potential, suggesting that there was a certain polarization in the redox process. The resulting curves of the peak current I p and v −1/2 were presented in Fig. 3h and Fig. 3i. The slopes of peaks 1–4 after fitting were 0.134, 0.191, 0.183, and 0.173, respectively. It can be seen that the composite material exhibits a good linear relationship, indicating that the sodium ion diffusion process controls the redox process of the material.

Fig. 4 (a) showed the charge–discharge curves of o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 during cycling at a current density of 500 mA g−1. Interestingly, even at such high current densities, the shape of the plateaus on the charge–discharge curves remains largely unchanged and the plateaus are still evident. This indicates that increasing the current density during cycling has little effect on the redox reaction of o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2. Compared with Fig. 4(b), the charge–discharge platform is still stable at 50 mA g−1. Fig. 4(c) results showed that o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 still exhibited excellent charge–discharge plateau characteristics. It is further explained that o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 can maintain excellent redox characteristics under different current densities in electrochemical performance tests. As performed in Fig. 4(d), the discharge specific capacities of o-Na2THBQ, o-Na2THBQ/CDs-1, o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2, and o-Na2THBQ/CDs-3 were 93, 153, 182 and 143 mAh g−1 after 500 cycles at 50 mA g−1, the electrochemical performance is significantly different. Among these materials, o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 exhibited enhanced electrochemical performance, and the charge–discharge efficiency was about 100 % after 500 cycles. Interestingly, Fig. 4(e) showed the long-term cycle stability of o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 at 500 mA g−1. Even after 1300 cycles, the composite maintained a specific discharge capacity of 63 mAh g−1, which is higher than that of similar materials, while also having a Coulombic efficiency of about 100 %. Some fluctuations in data may be associated with environmental factors. Fig. 4(f) depicted the step rate performance of o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2. When the current density was increased from 30 mA g−1 to 480 mA g−1, the specific capacities of o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 were 274, 233, 194, 147 and 90 mAh g−1, respectively. More intriguingly, the composite still maintained a specific capacity of 239 mAh g−1 as the current density returned to 30 mA g−1, indicating its good rate capability.

The sodium ion diffusion coefficient of the materials was tested by the galvanostatic intermittent titration technique (GITT). The voltage range for the GITT test is 1.0–3.0 V. This method was used to explore the speed of sodium ion diffusion in o-Na2THBQ and o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2. Before the GITT test, both materials were pre-cycled at 0.05 A g−1 and an interval of 0.5 h to achieve a stable battery working state. The discharge specific capacity can be seen in Fig. 5 (a), which is the charge–discharge curve of the two composites. The specific capacity of o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 composite is larger than that of o-Na2THBQ. The discharge specific capacity of o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 is 245 mAh g−1, while that of o-Na2THBQ is 148 mAh g−1. The relevant ΔES and ΔEτ values were obtained from the voltage–time curves. Fig. 5(b) and (e) dispalyed the voltage–time curves of o-Na2THBQ and o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2, respectively. The following formula calculates the diffusion coefficient of ions in the electrode [39]:
D
N
a +
=
4
π τ
(
m B
V M
M B
S
)
2
Δ
E S

( τ < <

D
Na +
)

where the relative molecular mass of the active material is MB , and the mass is expressed as mB , VM is the molar volume of the active material, ΔES and ΔEτ are the pulse-induced voltage changes, ΔEτ is the representative symbol of charge (discharge) voltage change at constant current, and S is the sum of all contact areas between the constituent electrolyte and electrode materials inside the battery.

Fig. 5(c) and (f) showed that the fifth charge–discharge cycle of o-Na2THBQ and o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2, respectively, the Na+ diffusion coefficient of o-Na2THBQ was in the range of 10−11 and 10−12 and that of o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 was 10−11, suggesting that the Na+ diffusion rate of o-Na2THBQ effectively increased after CDs composited, thereby, greatly improving the electrochemical performance.

To explore the mechanism of the cathode material o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 for sodium-ion batteries, ex-situ FT-IR measurements were elucidated. Fig. 6 (a) was the charge–discharge curve of o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 in the first cycle. Fig. 6(b) showed that the CO absorption curve at 1625 cm−1 gradually increased during charging and decreases during discharging. The CO absorption peak reaches the strongest at full charge to 3.0 V, indicating that Na+ is fully disengaged from the enol bond. During this period when a is fully used up to 1.0 V, the CO absorption peak at 1625 cm−1 not obvious, indicating that both CO are converted to enol bonds. When the battery was in the mid-charge state (voltage was 2.0 V), two bonds of CO (1625 cm−1) and CONa (1063 cm−1) coexisted [40], indicating that the redox reaction of o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 composite is essentially associated with CO. The charging-discharging mechanism is shown in Fig. 6(c), two CO on the benzene ring insert a Na+ during the discharge process to form an enol bond CONa, which is broken during the charging process, thereby, reforming CO again.

In this research, o-Na2THBQ/CDs composites are successfully synthesized by ultrasonic method. The introduction of CDs improved the electrochemical performance of o-Na2THBQ and suppressed the dissolution of o-Na2THBQ in organic electrolytes. The composite exhibits higher capacity and outstanding cycling properties when employed as a cathode for sodium-ion batteries. o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 the specific discharge capacity is about twice that of o-Na2THBQ. Results showed that the o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 composite demonstrated superior electrochemical performance among all materials. Not only that, the discharge specific capacity is 182 mAh g−1 after 500 cycles at 50 mA g−1. Interestingly, o-Na2THBQ/CDs-2 can be cycled 1300 times at 500 mA g−1. A high specific capacity of 63 mAh g−1 is also demonstrated in this study. The unique charging and discharging mechanism and superior performance are also discussed. This simple and effective composite strategy provides a new way to construct small organic molecules with enhanced electrochemical performance.

Youchi Ding: Conceptualization, Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Lingling Xie: Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – review & editing. Yuwei Zhang: Writing – review & editing. Xizhuo Chen: Writing – review & editing. Yu Niu: Writing – original draft, Investigation. Jing Xu: Resources, Investigation. Qing Han: Formal analysis, Investigation. Xuejing Qiu: Formal analysis, Investigation. Yongxia Miao: Visualization, Investigation. Yongmei Xiao: Conceptualization, Visualization, Investigation. Limin Zhu: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition. Xiaoyu Cao: Conceptualization, Validation, Resources, Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Project administration, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by National Natural Science Foundation, China (Nos. 52071132, U21A20284 and U1904216), Zhongyuan Thousand People Plan-The Zhongyuan Youth Talent Support Program (in Science and Technology), China (No. ZYQR201810139), Innovative Funds Plan of Henan University of Technology, China (No. 2020ZKCJ04), and Natural Science Foundation of Henan, China (No. 222300420138).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: H, Wang, Understanding the sodium storage mechanisms of organic electrodes in sodium-ion batteries: issues and solutions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: R. Rajagopalan, Y. Tang, C. Jia, X. Ji，H, Wang, Understanding the sodium storage mechanisms of organic electrodes in sodium-ion batteries: issues and solutions, Energ. Environ. Sci. 13 (2020) 1568−1592.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent progress in thermoelectric materials based on conjugated polymers

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: C. Yao, H. Zhang, Q. Zhang, Recent progress in thermoelectric materials basedon conjugated polymers, Polymers 11 (2019) 107.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Conjugated carbonyl compounds as high-performance cathode materials for rechargeable batteries

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: L. Zhu, G. Ding, L. Xie, X. Cao, J. Liu, X. Lei, J. Ma, Conjugated carbonyl compounds as high-performance cathode materials for rechargeable batteries, Chem. Mater. 31 (2019) 8582−8612.
